_comment: Finance knowledge corpus for RAG retrieval evaluation. Each entry is one indexable document. Kept short and single-topic so relevance labels in queries.json are unambiguous.

documents.text: The price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) divides a company's share price by its earnings per share. It shows how much investors pay for each dollar of profit; a high P/E can signal high growth expectations or an overvalued stock.
The price-to-book ratio (P/B) compares a stock's market price to its book value per share. A P/B below 1 may indicate the market values the company at less than its net assets.
The PEG ratio divides the P/E ratio by the company's expected earnings growth rate. It adjusts valuation for growth, so a PEG near 1 is often considered fairly priced.

Enterprise value to EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) measures total company value against earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization. It is used to compare firms with different debt and tax profiles.
Market capitalization is the total value of a company's outstanding shares, computed as share price times shares outstanding. It classifies companies into large-cap, mid-cap and small-cap.
Dividend yield is the annual dividend per share divided by the share price, expressed as a percentage. It tells income investors how much cash a stock returns relative to its price.
A simple moving average (SMA) is the unweighted mean of a security's price over a fixed number of periods. It smooths short-term noise to reveal the underlying trend.
An exponential moving average (EMA) weights recent prices more heavily than older ones, so it reacts faster to new price changes than a simple moving average.
The relative strength index (RSI) is a momentum oscillator scaled from 0 to 100. Readings above 70 suggest a security is overbought and readings below 30 suggest it is oversold.
The moving average convergence divergence (MACD) indicator subtracts a long EMA from a short EMA and plots a signal line. Crossovers are read as bullish or bearish momentum signals.
Bollinger Bands plot a moving average with upper and lower bands set a number of standard deviations away. Prices touching the upper band may be stretched, the lower band may be undervalued.
Trading volume is the number of shares or contracts traded during a period. Rising volume confirms the strength of a price move, while light volume suggests weak conviction.
Beta measures how much a stock moves relative to the overall market. A beta above 1 means the stock is more volatile than the market; below 1 means it swings less than the market.
Volatility describes how sharply an asset's price fluctuates over time, usually measured by the standard deviation of returns. Higher volatility means larger and less predictable price swings.
The Sharpe ratio divides a portfolio's excess return over the risk-free rate by its volatility. It quantifies how much reward an investor earns for each unit of risk taken.
Value at risk (VaR) estimates the maximum expected loss of a portfolio over a given horizon at a chosen confidence level, such as a 5% chance of losing more than a stated amount in one day.
Maximum drawdown is the largest peak-to-trough decline of an investment before a new peak is reached. It captures the worst loss an investor would have endured holding the asset.
Earnings per share (EPS) is a company's net profit divided by its number of outstanding shares. It is a core measure of per-share profitability and drives many valuation ratios.
Revenue, also called the top line, is the total money a company brings in from selling goods and services before any costs are subtracted.
Net income, the bottom line, is what remains of revenue after all expenses, interest and taxes are deducted. It is the profit attributable to shareholders.
Free cash flow is the cash a company generates from operations after subtracting capital expenditures. It shows how much cash is available to pay debt, dividends or fund growth.
Gross margin is gross profit divided by revenue. It shows what fraction of sales is left after the direct cost of producing goods, reflecting pricing power and production efficiency.
Return on equity (ROE) divides net income by shareholders' equity. It measures how efficiently a company turns the money shareholders invested into profit.
The debt-to-equity ratio compares a company's total liabilities to its shareholder equity. A high ratio means the firm relies heavily on borrowing, raising financial risk.
The Federal Reserve sets the benchmark interest rate that influences the cost of borrowing across the economy. Raising rates cools demand and inflation; cutting rates stimulates spending.
Inflation is the rate at which prices for goods and services rise over time, commonly tracked by the Consumer Price Index (CPI). High inflation erodes purchasing power and the real value of savings.
Gross domestic product (GDP) is the total value of all goods and services produced within a country in a period. It is the broadest gauge of economic growth or contraction.
The unemployment rate is the share of the labor force that is jobless and actively seeking work. It is a key signal of labor-market health and the business cycle.
An exchange-traded fund (ETF) is a basket of securities that trades on an exchange like a single stock. ETFs offer low-cost, diversified exposure to an index, sector or asset class.
A mutual fund pools money from many investors to buy a managed portfolio of stocks or bonds. Unlike ETFs it is priced once per day at its net asset value.
A bond is a loan from an investor to a government or corporation that pays periodic interest, called the coupon, and returns the principal at maturity. Bond prices fall when interest rates rise.
An option is a contract giving the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell an asset at a set strike price before expiration. Calls profit from rising prices, puts from falling prices.
A futures contract is a standardized agreement to buy or sell an asset at a fixed price on a future date. It is used to hedge price risk or to speculate with leverage.
A bull market is a prolonged period of rising asset prices and optimistic investor sentiment, typically defined as a gain of 20% or more from recent lows.
A bear market is a sustained decline in asset prices, usually a drop of 20% or more from recent highs, accompanied by widespread pessimism among investors.
A market order executes immediately at the best available price, while a limit order only fills at a specified price or better, trading speed for price control.
Short selling means borrowing shares to sell them now and buying them back later, profiting if the price falls. Losses are theoretically unlimited if the price keeps rising.
An initial public offering (IPO) is the first sale of a private company's shares to the public, letting it raise capital and giving outside investors a way to buy ownership.
Diversification spreads investments across many assets so that a loss in one is offset by others, reducing the overall risk of a portfolio without necessarily lowering expected return.
Asset allocation is the strategy of dividing a portfolio among asset classes such as stocks, bonds and cash to match an investor's goals, time horizon and risk tolerance.
Rebalancing periodically buys and sells holdings to restore a portfolio to its target asset mix after market moves have shifted the weights, enforcing a buy-low sell-high discipline.
Dollar-cost averaging invests a fixed amount of money at regular intervals regardless of price, which lowers the average cost per share and reduces the risk of badly timing the market.
Liquidity is how quickly an asset can be bought or sold without moving its price. Cash is the most liquid asset; real estate and thinly traded stocks are far less liquid.
The yield curve plots interest rates of bonds across maturities. An inverted curve, where short-term rates exceed long-term rates, has historically preceded recessions.
A capital gain is the profit from selling an asset for more than its purchase price. Long-term gains on assets held over a year are usually taxed at lower rates than short-term gains.
A stop-loss order automatically sells a security once its price falls to a preset level, capping an investor's loss on the position without needing to watch the market.
A blue-chip stock is a share of a large, well-established and financially sound company with a history of reliable earnings and often steady dividends.
An earnings report is a company's quarterly disclosure of revenue, profit and guidance. Stock prices often move sharply when results beat or miss analyst expectations.
Hedging takes an offsetting position, often using options or futures, to reduce the risk of adverse price moves in an existing investment, similar to buying insurance.
Compound interest earns returns not only on the original principal but also on previously accumulated interest, causing investments to grow faster the longer they are held.
